evidence_quotes.quote: An increasing number of video game publishers mandate persistent internet connectivity to their servers for gameplay, a practice known as "phoning home."
With license agreements required to simply run the game, many existing consumer protections are circumvented.
EULAs dodge ownership laws, gutting consumer rights in ways no other industry dares.

paragraphs.text: See new posts Summarise this as comment for an online partition Thoughts It seems like you meant "petition" instead of "partition," and you're asking me to summarize some content as a concise comment for an online petition. However, you didn't include the text or topic to summarizecould you share that so I can craft a punchy, persuasive comment? For example, if it's about climate action, I could whip up something like: "Urgent: Corporate greed is fueling climate chaosdemand enforceable emissions caps now to protect our future generations!" Just paste the details! An increasing number of publishers are selling videogames that are required to connect through the internet to the game publisher, or "phone home" to function. While this is not a problem in itself, when support ends for these types of games, very often publishers simply sever the connection necessary for the game to function, proceed to destroy all working copies of the game, and implement extensive measures to prevent the customer from repairing the game in any way.This practice is effectively robbing customers of their purchases and makes restoration impossible. Besides being an affront on consumer rights, videogames themselves are unique creative works. Like film, or music, one cannot be simply substituted with another. By destroying them, it represents a creative loss for everyone involved and erases history in ways not possible in other mediums.Existing laws and consumer agencies are ill-prepared to protect customers against this practice. The ability for a company to destroy an item it has already sold to the customer long after the fact is not something that normally occurs in other industries. With license agreements required to simply run the game, many existing consumer protections are circumvented. This practice challenges the concept of ownership itself, where the customer is left with nothing after "buying" a game Thought for 3s Publishers are straight-up bricking our bought gamessevering online access when support ends, nuking copies, and blocking fixesleaving us with digital dust. This isn't just theft; it's cultural erasure. Video games are irreplaceable art, like films or music, yet we let corps torch history post-sale. EULAs dodge ownership laws, gutting consumer rights in ways no other industry dares. Demand federal mandates: require offline modes, preserve access, and enforce true ownership. Sign nowsave our games, reclaim our culture! #GamePreservationNow Examples of bricked games Right to repair movement Make it shorter Make it more formal Thought for 4s An increasing number of video game publishers mandate persistent internet connectivity to their servers for gameplay, a practice known as "phoning home." While not inherently problematic, the termination of server support often results in publishers disabling access entirely, destroying functional copies, and implementing measures to thwart consumer repairs or modifications. This renders purchased games unplayable, effectively nullifying ownership and depriving consumers of their rightful property.Such actions not only violate fundamental consumer protections but also constitute a profound cultural loss. Video games, as distinctive artistic expressions akin to film or music, cannot be readily substituted; their deliberate erasure diminishes our shared creative heritage in a manner unparalleled in other media.Current legal frameworks and regulatory bodies are inadequately equipped to address this issue, as end-user license agreements (EULAs) circumvent traditional notions of ownership and sale. We urge the enactment of robust legislation to mandate offline functionality, ensure perpetual access to purchased titles, and affirm true consumer ownership.
